topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a sharp, unwelcome sound in the quiet hum of my laptop. My breath hitched. I blinked several times, rereading the screen. It was Professor Albright’s profile picture, superimposed over a recent post about a historical documentary. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, suddenly slick with sweat. Every muscle in my jaw tightened. Who was he tagging? Why was this visible to me? A cold knot began to tighten in my stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. I quickly minimized the window, shoving the laptop under my textbook, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. The silence of the room felt suddenly suffocating, amplifying the frantic thumping inside my chest.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at my phone screen, the brightness seeming too harsh in the dim afternoon light. A picture of me, taken in the library, was shared by Mr. Davies. Beside it, a caption referencing a recent class discussion. My hands started to tremble, a fine vibration running up my forearms. I kept scrolling, a desperate need to confirm or deny what my eyes were telling me. Each new piece of information only seemed to ratchet up the internal pressure building behind my eyes. I leaned back in my chair, the wood groaning softly under my weight, and squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to steady the rapid rhythm of my pulse. The reality of the situation felt too large, too invasive.

[story 3]

It happened during the late-night study session. I was reviewing notes when a notification popped up from my private account. It was a direct message from the teacher. My vision blurred slightly as I read the few words. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I instinctively pulled my hoodie tighter around my neck, as if the fabric could somehow shield me from the sudden exposure. My throat felt dry and tight. I dropped my pen; it clattered loudly on the desk, a sound that seemed deafening in the stillness. I looked around the empty room, suddenly hyper-aware of every shadow, every creak of the building. I needed to move, to escape the immediate vicinity of the screen, but my feet felt rooted to the floor.
